# Transcrição: 01-Story- 'Matty the Dog'

- **Fonte:** 01-Story- 'Matty the Dog'
- **Duração:** 06:01
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:04]

It's the summer of 2003. I'm 32 years old. I'm sitting in my cruddy little apartment at my cruddy little kitchen table and I'm pouring water over raisin bran because I'm too stupid and I'm too sad to go get milk. It's the first time in my life I'm living alone. And I've discovered that when men live alone, they become heathens. There are days when I just forget to go to sleep. There was an afternoon where I found myself walking through my apartment wearing underwear and one sock only and I couldn't tell you how that happened. I've been in this apartment for about three months. I have yet to change the sheets on my bed and I really can't foresee a time when those sheets will get changed. I have yet to put a shower curtain in the bathroom so that when I take a shower, the whole bathroom takes a shower with me. I hate this apartment. I hate my life. I hate everything about it. I'm living above a family of Indians who cook everything in curry and our air conditioners are stacked one on top of each other. So as their curry smell wafts out of their apartment, my air conditioner sucks it in. So everything in my apartment smells like curry. My sheets smell like curry. My pillow smells like curry. My clothing smells like curry. Even my dog smells like curry. And I hate curry. Less than a mile away, my wife is probably sitting in our kitchen right now in the house we bought together. She's probably sitting at the table we bought a day after we bought the house or maybe she's out on the deck, the deck I put in last summer. And by put in, I really mean I just paid a couple of guys to do it, but I paid the money and watched them do it so I'm going to take credit for putting it in. We're separated. We've been separated for a little while now and I'm worried that it might be leading to something else. About a year ago, my wife asked me for space and I didn't really know what that meant. So I started sitting on the other side of the couch when we watched TV. It turns out that's not exactly what she was looking for. About six months ago, she told me that she was bored in our relationship and that was ridiculous. I am a lot of things. I am pedantic and obsessive and organized and perhaps a little neurotic, but I am not boring. I'm a wedding DJ. My partner, my DJ partner, Benji and I, we go out on weekends and entertain crowds. Just a couple months ago, I ran into a couple who's wedding I DJed and they told me they bought a dog and they named their dog Maddie because I was the funnest person they'd ever known. Verifiable proof that I am not a boring person. And mind you, my DJ partner's name is Benji. They could have named the dog after the guy who was named after the dog, but they named their dog after me. I am not a boring person. But now we're separated. We've been separated for a while and I can see that this is probably leading to divorce. And I can't believe this is happening to me. My parents got divorced when I was a kid and it really ruined enormous portions of my childhood.

## [03:04 - 06:01]

And so when I was a kid, I promised myself that when I grew up and I got married, I would never allow divorce to enter my life, that I would find the right person and I would work as hard as it took to make sure that marriage stuck. And now I'm here losing the person who I thought I would love forever. I've never been depressed in my life, but for the first time, I'm starting to feel the creeping sadness encroach upon everything around me. And so Benji and I go to Vermont for the weekend. We go to DJ a wedding. And when I come back, I'm pulling into the parking lot of my apartment complex and I can see that there are two moving vans up against the building. The Indian family is moving out. One good thing in my life, no more curry smell in my house. So I'm thrilled. I see they're trying to get a couch sort of out the doorway and they're struggling with it. And I can see that there's another moving van right next to it. It's the people who are going to move in right underneath me as soon as this family is gone. And as I walk up the walk, I see the woman standing on the top of the van and I recognize her. I know who she is. And then I hear her call my name. Hi, Maddie. It's the bride. It's the bride who named her dog Maddie. And then I see the dog. I can't believe it. The dog is coming around the truck. The dog named after me is going to live below me. This is amazing. And then I see the husband come around holding the leash, except that's not the husband. Who is that? I know that guy too. And then I realize the best man. And so the bride and the best man approach and I say hello and I say hi and I look back to her and she says, yeah. And I can't believe it. And so I tell them that if they need some help moving in, I live upstairs. I'm going to live above the dog named after me. And so I climb the stairs to my apartment, my cruddy little apartment, and I sit at my cruddy kitchen table. But for the first time in a long time, I feel a lot better about my life because somewhere in the world tonight, there's a man and he's lost his wife and his best friend and his dog. And I may be losing my wife, a wife that I probably never loved enough to begin with, but I still have my best friend and I still have my dog. And I may be 32 years old, but that is the first moment in a very long time that I feel like there might be a future for me after all. Thank you.
